title.en: Mādhava and the Kerala school — infinite series

dating.display: Mādhava fl. c. 1340–1425 CE; key surviving expositions: Nīlakaṇṭha's Tantrasaṅgraha (1501) and Jyeṣṭhadeva's Yuktibhāṣā (c. 1530)
dating.basis: The school's lineage and dates are well anchored by the texts themselves and their astronomical epochs. Mādhava's own works on series are lost; his results survive with explicit attribution and full proofs in successor texts.

summary: An unbroken teacher-student lineage in Kerala (Mādhava → Parameśvara → Dāmodara → Nīlakaṇṭha → Jyeṣṭhadeva...) that derived infinite series for π (the 'Mādhava–Leibniz' series, with error-correction terms that make it actually converge usefully), and power series for sine and cosine ('Mādhava–Newton' series) — with proofs, in the Yuktibhāṣā, that proceed by summing vanishing subdivisions.
whyItMatters: Core results of early calculus — infinite series expansions of trigonometric functions — documented in India two to three centuries before Newton, Leibniz and Gregory, with attribution chains preserved inside the tradition itself. Recognized in mainstream history of mathematics (Katz, Plofker).
honestyNote: Two separate claims must not be merged. (1) PRIORITY of the series results in Kerala: documented, mainstream. (2) TRANSMISSION of those results to Europe via Jesuits in Malabar: a hypothesis with circumstantial evidence only — no manuscript trail has been found. The through-line marks (2) as conjectural. Asserting (2) as fact is a category-9 overclaim; dismissing (1) because of (2) is a category-6 misattribution.

locations.name: Saṅgamagrāma (identified with the Irinjalakuda region, Thrissur, Kerala, India)

throughLines.to: Calculus — infinite series for π and trigonometric functions
chain: Mādhava (c. 1400): series for π with rational error terms; sine/cosine power series (attributed by name in successor texts)
Jyeṣṭhadeva, Yuktibhāṣā (c. 1530): complete derivations — a proof text, not just results
Whish (1834) introduces the corpus to European scholarship; 20th-c. history of mathematics (Rajagopal, Katz, Plofker) establishes priority

sources.label: Katz, V., "Ideas of Calculus in Islam and India", Mathematics Magazine 68:3 (1995)
Sarma, K.V. (ed./tr.), Gaṇita-Yukti-Bhāṣā of Jyeṣṭhadeva, 2 vols. (Springer, 2008)
Whish, C.M., "On the Hindú Quadrature of the Circle...", Transactions of the Royal Asiatic Society 3 (1834)

chain: Jesuit missionaries active in Malabar from the 1540s, some collecting local astronomy for calendar reform
Hypothesis (Raju; Almeida & Joseph): Kerala results reached Europe and influenced 17th-c. developments
Status: no documentary chain located; mainstream assessment is 'possible, unproven'

sources.label: Raju, C.K., Cultural Foundations of Mathematics (Pearson, 2007) — the transmission thesis (minority position, labeled as such)
Plofker, K., Mathematics in India (2009) — mainstream assessment of the transmission question
Plofker, K., Mathematics in India (2009), ch. 7
Joseph, G.G., The Crest of the Peacock: Non-European Roots of Mathematics, 3rd ed. (Princeton, 2011)

image.alt: Mādhava and the Kerala school — infinite series
